contexts.text: Asymptotic stationarity and ergodicity are key assumptions needed to make it possible to learn by applying statistical methods.
Answering these questions will lead us to revisit many of the topics that occupied us in the finite state case, such as simulation, distribution dynamics, stability, ergodicity, etc.
In our lecture on finite Markov chains, we also studied stationarity, stability and ergodicity.
So what are these conditions we require to get global stability and ergodicity?
Under very similar conditions, we get *ergodicity*, which means that for any (measurable) function   such that the right-hand side is finite.
Furthermore, when the Markov chain  and the government expenditure function  are such that  is perpetually random,  almost surely converges to zero.

This lecture studies a special  AMSS model in which * The exogenous state variable  is governed by  a finite-state Markov chain. * With an arbitrary budget-feasible initial level of government debt, the measurability  constraints - bind for many periods, but . - eventually, they stop binding evermore, so that - in the tail of the Ramsey plan, the Lagrange multipliers  on the AMSS implementability constraints {eq}`TS_gov_wo4`  are zero. * After the implementability constraints {eq}`TS_gov_wo4` no longer bind in the tail of the AMSS Ramsey plan - history dependence of the AMSS state variable  vanishes and   becomes a time-invariant function of the Markov state . - the par value of government debt becomes **constant over time** so that  for  for a sufficiently large . - , so that the tail of the Ramsey plan instructs  the government always to make a constant par value of risk-free one-period loans **to** the private sector. - the one-period gross interest rate  on risk-free debt  converges to a time-invariant function of the Markov state . * For a **particular**  (i.e., a positive level of initial government **loans** to the private sector), the measurability constraints **never** bind. * In this special case - the **par value**   of government debt at time  and Markov state   is constant across time and states, but . - the **market value**  of government debt at time   varies as a time-invariant function of the Markov state . - fluctuations in the interest rate make gross earnings on government debt  fully insure the gross-of-gross-interest-payments government budget against fluctuations in government expenditures. - the state variable  in a recursive representation of a Ramsey plan is a time-invariant function of the Markov state for . * In this special case, the Ramsey allocation in the AMSS model agrees with that in a Lucas-Stokey {cite}`LucasStokey1983` complete markets model in which the same amount of state-contingent debt falls due in all states tomorrow - it is a situation in which  the Ramsey planner loses nothing from not being  able to  trade state-contingent debt and being restricted to exchange only risk-free debt  debt. * This outcome emerges only when we initialize government debt at a particular .

Evidently, as the Markov chain becomes *more nearly periodic* (i.e., as ), the dynamic program adjusts capital faster in the low adjustment cost Markov state to take advantage of what is only temporarily a more favorable time to invest.
The idea is to replace the constant matrices that define a **linear quadratic dynamic programming problem** with  sets of matrices that are fixed functions of the state of an  state Markov chain.

We use it to convey the heart of what BCG call a **rational conjectures** equilibrium in which conjectures are about equilibrium pricing functions in regions of the state space that  an average consumer or firm does not visit in equilibrium.

Since this object is an instance of MarkovChain from  QuantEcon.py (see this lecture for more discussion), we can easily simulate it, compute its stationary distribution and so on.
In the finite case, we learned that at least one stationary distribution exists, although there may be many.
A stationary distribution is a distribution that is a "fixed point" of the update rule for the AR(1) process.
In particular, averages over time series converge to expectations under the stationary distribution.

So Perron-Frobenius theorem explains why both {ref}`Imam and Temple matrix ` and {ref}`Hamilton matrix ` converge to a stationary distribution, which is the Perron projection of the two matrices We can also verify other properties hinted by Perron-Frobenius in these stochastic matrices. +++ Another example is the relationship between convergence gap and convergence rate.
In the previous section, we made the connection between stochastic difference equation {eq}`statd_rw` and stochastic kernel {eq}`statd_rwsk`.

Thus, by converting a stochastic difference equation such as {eq}`statd_srs` into a stochastic kernel  and hence an operator , we convert a stochastic difference equation into a deterministic one (albeit in a much higher dimensional space).

Let's start with some imports: The **AR(1) model** (autoregressive model of order 1) takes the form where  are scalar-valued parameters (Equation {eq}`can_ar1` is sometimes called a **stochastic difference equation**.) The specification {eq}`can_ar1` generates a time series  as soon as we specify an initial condition .

We create a *random* or *stochastic* version of the model by adding a random process of *shocks* or *disturbances* to the right side of equation {eq}`second_order`, leading to the *second-order scalar linear stochastic difference equation*: To get started, let's set , , and .

The {ref}`*steady state*` value  of  is obtained by setting $y_t = y_{t-1} = y_{t-2} = y^*$ in {eq}`tswm_1`, which yields If we set the initial values to , then  will be constant: To generate some excitement, we'll follow in the spirit of the great economists Eugen Slutsky and Ragnar Frisch and replace our original second-order difference equation with the following **second-order stochastic linear difference equation**: where  and is {ref}`IID `, meaning independent and identically distributed.

This modification makes national output become governed by a second-order **stochastic linear difference equation** that, with appropriate parameter values, gives rise to recurrent irregular business cycles. (To read about stochastic linear difference equations see chapter XI of {cite}`Sargent1987`.) As we have seen, the largest eigenvalue for a primitive stochastic matrix is one.

One of the advantages of this alternative set up is that it permits the use of a sparse matrix for `Q`. (An example of using sparse matrices is given in the exercises below) The call signature of the second formulation is `DiscreteDP(R, Q, β, s_indices, a_indices)` where * `s_indices` and `a_indices` are arrays of equal length `L` enumerating all feasible state-action pairs * `R` is an array of length `L` giving corresponding rewards * `Q` is an `L x n` transition probability array Here's how we could set up these objects for the preceding example For larger problems, you might need to write this code more efficiently by vectorizing or using Numba.

The transition probability array `Q` will be highly sparse (in fact it is degenerate as the model is deterministic), so we formulate the problem with state-action pairs, to represent `Q` in [scipy sparse matrix format](http://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/sparse.html).
